topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I was making coffee, the aroma of burnt grounds usually a comforting morning ritual, but today it just felt sharp and accusing. Tucked beneath a stack of textbooks on the desk, I found it: my journal. Not just open, but actively being read. The pages were turned with a deliberate, almost reverent slowness, the kind of attention you usually reserve for something deeply personal. My breath hitched in my throat, a tight, shallow catch. I froze, my hand hovering over the mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling hot and alien in my grip. When I finally dared to look up, his eyes were fixed on the script, and a flicker—was it guilt? Regret?—crossed his face before he quickly looked away, a flush creeping up his neck. I didn't say anything. I just stood there, the silence in the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest, feeling utterly exposed under the harsh fluorescent light.

[story 2]

I found the evidence while I was tidying up after a late-night study session. My journal, the one filled with rambling thoughts about exams and anxieties, was open on the bedside table. The handwriting was familiar, undeniably mine, but the words spilling across the page were being traced with a fingertip, lingering on certain passages. A cold dread washed over me, making my fingers tingle. I slowly approached the desk, my movements stiff, every muscle tense. When I saw him, hunched over the book, his posture seemed smaller than usual, shoulders slightly slumped. He flinched violently when he realized I was watching, quickly snapping the book shut as if catching himself in a transgression. His voice, when he finally spoke, was unusually quiet, laced with a strained defensiveness that didn't quite mask something heavy underneath. I just nodded once, my gaze dropping to the floor, the weight of the situation settling heavily onto my shoulders.

[story 3]

It was late, the kind of quiet hour when the campus seemed to exhale, leaving only the hum of the refrigerator. I was looking for my headphones and stumbled upon it—my private thoughts laid bare. The shock wasn't immediate; it was a slow, creeping realization that something deeply private had been invaded. My stomach clenched, a tight knot of unease tightening around my ribs. When I saw him sitting in the armchair across the room, illuminated by the soft glow of his laptop screen, his attention was entirely focused on the open book. He didn't look up immediately, just a slight, almost imperceptible tightening around his jaw. When he finally lifted his head, his expression was a complicated mix of vulnerability and something guarded, like he was bracing for an accusation. I felt a profound sense of awkwardness, a desperate urge to retreat, unable to form a coherent response to the quiet tension radiating between us.
